answer: The couplet is from chapter 5 of Diwan-E-Zauq (sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798): 'Ae Zauq waqt naale ke rakh le jigar pe haath / Warna jigar ko roye-ga tu dhar ke sar pe haath' ('O Zauq, at the moment of complaint/lament, keep your hand upon your heart (jigar) / Or else you will weep for your heart, hands raised in grief'). The poetic image is that the breath at the moment of complaint (naala — the wailing breath) burns the jigar (liver/heart) like a flame; therefore one must hold one's hand over it to contain the fire. The 'breath of complaint' (waqt naale ke) is implicitly compared to a flame on the heart (jigar pe), which is the classical Urdu trope underlying the instruction.
